did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a hybrid AI approach combining 2D Wavelet Transform for feature extraction and a Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN) implemented in NEURAL LAB software to classify dermoscopic images as malignant or benign skin cancer.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' and presents original experimental work. It details a specific methodology involving a hybrid approach of 2D Wavelet Transform and Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN) implemented in NEURAL LAB software. The study includes a dataset of 31 dermoscopic images, describes the experimental setup (training/testing split, epochs, learning rate), and reports specific classification results and a confusion matrix, indicating it produces new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: AI-Driven Early Detection of Skin Cancer in the USA: A Hybrid Image Processing and Neural Network Approach
journal: Journal of Medical and Health Studies
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid AI-driven approach for the early detection of skin cancer using dermoscopic images. The methodology involves preprocessing images via median filtering and adaptive contrast enhancement, followed by segmentation using thresholding. A 2D Wavelet Transform is employed to extract five key features (Mean, Standard Deviation, Mean Absolute Deviation, L1 Norm, and L2 Norm) from the segmented lesions. These features are classified as malignant or benign using a Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN) implemented in NEURAL LAB software. The model was evaluated on a dataset of 31 dermoscopic images obtained from the internet, with 21 instances used for classification. The results demonstrated that the proposed computer-based diagnostic method achieves commendable precision and offers a non-invasive alternative to traditional biopsy techniques.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as class '1' in the binary classification task. The study focuses on distinguishing malignant melanoma from benign conditions using a dataset of 31 dermoscopic images (21 used for classification).
Classified as class '0' in the binary classification task. Referred to as 'benign melanoma' and 'benign tumors' in the text, serving as the comparator to malignant melanoma.

all_task_types.details: The model performs binary classification to distinguish between malignant melanoma (class '1') and benign/non-cancerous lesions (class '0'). The output layer generates discrete binary outputs (0 or 1) using a linear activation function, and the study reports a confusion matrix for classification performance.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopic Image Collection
datasets_used.description: A collection of 31 dermoscopic images obtained from publicly accessible dermatology databases and internet archives. The images were validated by expert annotation and adjusted to a uniform resolution of 360x360 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN) with a 70:30 split for training and testing sets. Specifically, 21 instances were provided for classification/training purposes in the final model evaluation.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance, with the dataset divided into training and testing sets in a 70:30 proportion.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the classification performance of the hybrid AI model by comparing predicted labels against expert-annotated ground truth for malignant and benign lesions.
Used as the loss function during the training of the Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN) to minimize the difference between predicted and actual outputs.
Used for normalizing the extracted statistical features (Mean, Standard Deviation, Mean Absolute Deviation, L1 Norm, L2 Norm) to ensure consistency in input data for the neural network.
To divide the dataset of 31 dermoscopic images into training and testing sets for model development and performance assessment.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using a confusion matrix and descriptive accuracy metrics.

Custom Dermoscopic Image Collection (31.notes: The study utilized 31 dermoscopic images, with 21 instances provided for classification (13 malignant, 8 benign). The model achieved a 'commendable level of precision' as described in the text. Specific feature values for each of the 21 instances are provided in Table I of the paper.

architecture_used.main_model: Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN) / Feedforward Multilayer Artificial Neural Network (ANN)
architecture_used.input_size: 5 features (Mean, Standard Deviation, Mean Absolute Deviation, L1 Norm, L2 Norm) extracted via 2D Wavelet Transform from 360x360 pixel dermoscopic images

architecture_details.activation_function: Linear activation function in the output layer

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Claret's hybrid model (DWT features with CNN)
Gessert et al.'s hybrid model (Gabor wavelets and small CNN ensembles)
Mahbod et al.'s hybrid deep neural network
preprocessing_techniques: Noise reduction via median filtering to diminish noise while maintaining edge integrity
Morphological operations (dilation and erosion) to remove small hairs and bubbles
Contrast enhancement techniques to highlight lesion characteristics
Threshold segmentation (binarization) using dynamically determined threshold values based on image intensity histograms
Post-segmentation morphological techniques to enhance lesion boundaries and eliminate minor noise abnormalities
Min-max scaling normalization of extracted statistical features
Resizing/adjustment to a uniform resolution of 360x360 pixels
conclusions: The proposed hybrid AI-driven methodology, combining 2D Wavelet Transform for feature extraction and a Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN) for classification, demonstrates a convenient level of accuracy for the early identification of skin cancer.
The computer-based diagnostic approach is superior to traditional biopsy techniques as it is non-invasive, eliminating the need for skin excision and relying solely on dermoscopic images.
The model successfully classified 21 dermoscopic instances, identifying 13 as malignant (cancerous) and 8 as non-cancerous (benign), achieving a commendable level of precision.
The study highlights the potential of altering and integrating these AI methods to enhance biomedical advancement in cancer detection systems.
The approach addresses limitations of conventional diagnostics by offering a non-invasive, rapid, and cost-effective alternative to invasive biopsies and subjective visual evaluations.
important_remarks: The study relies on a critically small dataset of only 31 dermoscopic images, with just 21 instances used for classification (13 malignant, 8 benign), which is insufficient for robust model validation or generalization.

The dataset was sourced from 'publicly accessible dermatology databases and internet archives' without specifying standard benchmarks (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000), raising concerns about data provenance, quality control, and potential selection bias.

The paper does not report standard classification metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, or AUC; it only provides a qualitative description ('commendable level of precision') and a confusion matrix without numerical values for true/false positives/negatives.
No external validation was performed; the model was trained and tested on the same small, non-standard dataset using a 70:30 split, increasing the risk of overfitting and data leakage.
The study lacks statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) and does not compare its performance against established baselines or dermatologist-level performance.
The claim of applicability to the 'USA healthcare landscape' is unsupported by any demographic data, geographic diversity, or clinical trial evidence within the study.

The methodology uses a simple Backpropagation Neural Network with only 2 hidden neurons, which is a very low-capacity model compared to modern deep learning standards, potentially limiting its ability to capture complex lesion features.

The paper acknowledges that AI models cannot perform physical examinations or patient interviews, yet concludes the method is 'superior' to biopsy without addressing the clinical necessity of histopathological confirmation for definitive diagnosis.
